schools.summary: Gill: Abib, sometimes called Nisan, answered to part of March and April, named from the corn appearing in ear, appointed the first of the months; in it, by night, the LORD brought them out of Egypt.

themes: Observe the month of Abib, and keep the passover... for in the month of Abib the LORD... brought thee forth out of Egypt by night
the yearly remembrance of redemption
Christ our passover sacrificed for us (1 Cor. 5:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the passover, a lamb killed on the fourteenth at night between the two evenings, was typical of Christ, the passover sacrificed for us.
Keil & Delitzsch: the sacrifice at the place which the LORD shall choose.

themes: Thou shalt therefore sacrifice the passover unto the LORD thy God... in the place which the LORD shall choose
the lamb typical of Christ
the one sacrifice, the Lamb of God (John 1:29)

schools.summary: Gill: no leavened bread was to be eaten with the passover.
Clarke: the unleavened 'bread of affliction' was unsavoury, putting them in mind of their afflictive bondage and the haste of their departure.

themes: seven days shalt thou eat unleavened bread... even the bread of affliction... that thou mayest remember
the memorial of bondage and haste
the gospel call to purge the old leaven (1 Cor. 5:8)

schools.summary: Gill: before the passover they were to search diligently every room, hole, and crevice, that no leaven remain anywhere.
Wesley: 'any of the flesh' is of the passover properly so called.

themes: there shall be no leavened bread seen with thee in all thy coasts seven days
the diligent search for every particle
the putting-away of sin entire (2 Cor. 7:1)

schools.summary: Gill: it might not be sacrificed within any of their gates or cities, but only in Jerusalem, the place the Lord chose, where alone they might kill and eat it.

themes: Thou mayest not sacrifice the passover within any of thy gates
the unity of worship at the one chosen place
one way of approach to God (Acts 4:12)

schools.summary: Gill: the place where God set his name, the ark and mercy-seat, his Shechinah dwelling — Jerusalem, where Solomon built the temple.
Wesley: it was offered in the court of the tabernacle or temple.

themes: there thou shalt sacrifice the passover at even... at the season that thou camest forth out of Egypt
the time and place fixing a sacred re-enactment
the redemption vividly renewed

schools.summary: Gill: the word for 'roast' can mean 'boil,' but the passover lamb was not to be boiled (expressly forbidden); they turned in the morning to their tents.

themes: thou shalt roast and eat it in the place which the LORD thy God shall choose... turn in the morning
the manner part of the type, roasted with fire
worship reverent and regulated

schools.summary: Gill: elsewhere it is seven days; the seventh was not exempt from unleavened bread, but distinguished for its special service, a solemn assembly with no work done.

themes: on the seventh day shall be a solemn assembly to the LORD thy God; thou shalt do no work
the feast crowned with holy convocation and rest
redemption issuing in worship (Heb. 10:25)

schools.summary: Gill: after this counting came the feast of weeks, called Pentecost from the fiftieth day. Calvin: the Passover fell when the harvests were beginning to ripen, the firstfruits then offered.

themes: Seven weeks shalt thou number... from such time as thou beginnest to put the sickle to the corn
the feast of weeks bound to the firstfruits
fulfilled at Pentecost (Acts 2:1)

schools.summary: Gill: the feast of Pentecost, at which the Spirit was poured down on the apostles.
Keil & Delitzsch: the tribute of a freewill offering of the hand, given as the LORD had blessed.

themes: thou shalt keep the feast of weeks... with a tribute of a freewill offering of thine hand
true worship a glad, free gift
giving as the LORD hath prospered (1 Cor. 16:2)

schools.summary: Gill: they were to make a liberal feast and keep it cheerfully before God, thankfully acknowledging his mercies.
Clarke: the offerings were eaten with festivity and shared with friends in liberality.

themes: thou shalt rejoice before the LORD thy God, thou... and the Levite... and the stranger, and the fatherless, and the widow
worship, joy, and charity wed
the festal gladness widened to the poor (Neh. 8:10)

schools.summary: Gill: the remembrance of their deliverance from bondage should make them liberal in their freewill offering, generous in the feast, and compassionate to the stranger, widow, and fatherless.

themes: thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in Egypt: and thou shalt observe and do these statutes
the memory of redemption the spring of compassion
the redeemed made generous and tender (2 Cor. 8:9)

schools.summary: Gill: it began on the fifteenth of Tisri (September). Calvin: its first day was the day of ingathering, when the produce of the whole year was stored in their granaries.

themes: Thou shalt observe the feast of tabernacles seven days, after that thou hast gathered in thy corn and thy wine
thanksgiving for the ingathering
looking on to the great ingathering (Rev. 7:9)

schools.summary: Gill: they were to rejoice at the feast of ingathering in gratitude for God's goodness; the Targum adds, with flute and pipe, music increasing the joy.
Keil & Delitzsch: the rejoicing of all, with the needy included.

themes: thou shalt rejoice in thy feast, thou, and thy son... and the Levite, the stranger, and the fatherless, and the widow
thanksgiving and charity wed
the festal gladness widened to all (Phil. 4:4)

schools.summary: Gill: this is the feast of tabernacles, kept seven days unto the LORD; the blessing of God on their increase and works was the ground of their rejoicing.

themes: because the LORD thy God shall bless thee in all thine increase... therefore thou shalt surely rejoice
joy a commanded duty
thankful gladness the answer to his bounty (Deut. 28:47)

schools.summary: Gill: repeated here for the sake of mentioning the place where they were to appear.
Clarke: refers to the three appearances (Exod. 23:17).

themes: Three times in a year shall all thy males appear before the LORD... and they shall not appear before the LORD empty
regular, costly worship at the chosen place
none approaching God without a gift (Ps. 96:8)

schools.summary: Gill: the quantity is not fixed in the law but left to the generosity of the people, the rule being that they give according to their ability as the Lord had prospered them.

themes: Every man shall give as he is able, according to the blessing of the LORD thy God
giving in proportion to ability
the gift measured by the blessing received (1 Cor. 16:2)

schools.summary: Gill: judges were fixed in the courts of judicature, and officers the executors who stood before them, ordering weights and measures and punishing wrongdoers; they were to judge with just judgment.

themes: Judges and officers shalt thou make thee in all thy gates... and they shall judge the people with just judgment
God's care for righteous government
rulers his ministers for justice (Rom. 13:4)

schools.summary: Gill: said to the judges as a direction — not to wrest or pervert judgment, nor pass a wrong sentence.
Keil & Delitzsch: not to wrest judgment, respect persons, or take a gift, for a gift blinds the eyes of the wise.

themes: Thou shalt not wrest judgment... neither take a gift: for a gift doth blind the eyes of the wise
justice guarded from its corrupters
an incorruptible judgment, free of bribe and favour (2 Chron. 19:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the Hebrew is 'justice, justice' — strict justice, and nothing else. Calvin: the emphatic repetition inculcates that judges should study equity with inflexible constancy, nothing being more likely to swerve them.

themes: That which is altogether just shalt thou follow, that thou mayest live, and inherit the land
the emphatic charge to inflexible justice
righteousness the foundation of a people's welfare (Prov. 21:3)

schools.summary: Gill: it was lawful to plant trees and groves, but not for a religious or idolatrous use.
Clarke: groves were planted about idol-temples for idolatrous purposes.

themes: Thou shalt not plant thee a grove of any trees near unto the altar of the LORD
God's worship kept clear of idolatry's forms
shunning the apparatus of false religion (1 Thess. 5:22)

schools.summary: Gill: the word signifies a statue or pillar set up for idolatry; what was not for idolatry, as a memorial, was not forbidden; the LORD hates every kind of idolatry.

themes: Neither shalt thou set thee up any image; which the LORD thy God hateth
idolatry not merely forbidden but hateful to God
his people to hate what he hates (Jer. 44:4)
